aliases: Black Tea with Sugar on Ice

english: Black Tea with Sugar on Ice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'black tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific entry for sweetened iced black tea or Teh O exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KN covers black tea infusion generically. No specific code for sweetened iced black tea (Teh O) exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227432007 refers to black tea as a substance. No specific concept for sweetened iced black tea beverage exists; closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup of Ice Teh O is approximately 500 ml, typical of a large kopitiam or café serving in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Teh O (Black Tea with Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Beverages, tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water; sugar addition estimated from standard kopitiam preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black tea per 100 ml from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 171917) provides the base nutrient profile (energy ~1 kcal/100 ml, protein ~0 g, fat 0 g, carbohydrate ~0.2 g). Sugar content is estimated from standard Singapore kopitiam Teh O preparation: approximately 2 teaspoons (8 g) of sugar per 200 ml serving, yielding ~4 g sugar per 100 ml of the final beverage. HPB Singapore Foodo confirms Teh O (sweetened black tea) as a standard beverage category. Final per-100 ml values: energy ~17 kcal, carbohydrate ~4.2 g (from sugar), protein ~0 g, fat 0 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — sucrose GI reference; sweetened black tea analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Teh O (sweetened iced black tea). The GI is estimated from the primary glycemic carbohydrate source: sucrose (table sugar), which has a GI of 65 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Plain black tea has negligible glycemic carbohydrates and does not materially alter the GI. The effective GI of the beverage is driven by the dissolved sucrose; a conservative estimate of 55 is applied (slightly below pure sucrose GI of 65) to account for the dilution effect and the modest tea matrix. Glycemic load per 500 ml large serving = (55 × 21.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 11.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~15 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Ice Teh O is very low in sodium (~15 mg per large serving) and poses minimal direct risk for hypertension; however, caffeine from black tea may cause a transient blood pressure rise and individuals with caffeine sensitivity should moderate intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (sucrose) contributing ~21 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate per 500 ml large serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Each large serving of Ice Teh O contains approximately 21 g of added sugar. While its glycemic index is low (55), the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~11.6), which can still cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced sugar or substitute with a sugar-free version.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat and zero cholesterol; moderate added sugar intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ice Teh O contains no fat or cholesterol and is low in sodium, making it broadly acceptable for cardiovascular health; the added sugar content should be moderated as part of an overall low-sugar dietary pattern to reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ice Teh O (Large) is a popular Singaporean kopitiam beverage consisting of freshly brewed black tea sweetened with white sugar and served over ice in a large cup (approximately 500 ml). 'Teh' is the Hokkien and Malay word for tea, and 'O' indicates that no milk is added, distinguishing it from 'Teh' (with condensed milk) or 'Teh C' (with evaporated milk). The beverage is a staple of Singapore's coffee shop (kopitiam) culture and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and café chains such as Toast Box and Ya Kun Kaya Toast. The flavour is clean, mildly astringent, and refreshing, with a sweet finish from the dissolved sugar. The tea base is typically a robust Ceylon or Assam black tea blend. Nutritionally, Ice Teh O is low in calories (~85 kcal per large serving), contains no fat, no protein, and no dietary fibre, but delivers approximately 21 g of added sugar per large serving, which is nutritionally significant. The glycemic index is low (55) but the glycemic load is moderate (~11.6 per serving), driven entirely by the sucrose content. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal diets. Individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome should be aware of the sugar content and consider requesting a reduced-sugar or sugar-free preparation. The beverage contains naturally occurring caffeine and polyphenols from black tea, which may offer modest antioxidant benefits.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Ice Teh O large?
Is Teh O suitable for diabetics?
How much sugar is in a large Teh O?
What is the difference between Teh and Teh O?
Glycemic index of sweetened iced black tea

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Ice Teh O Large' was found with a specific per-100 ml nutrient panel. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy plain brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917) with sugar addition estimated from standard Singapore kopitiam preparation practice (~2 tsp sugar per 200 ml). HPB Singapore Foodo confirms Teh O as a standard beverage category. GI is an analogous estimate based on sucrose GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero: plain brewed black tea contains no protein, and no protein-contributing ingredients are added. 2. fat_g is naturally zero: black tea and sugar contain no fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171917). 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are all naturally zero for the same reason. 4. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: brewed tea is a liquid infusion with no fibre-contributing solids. 5. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero: no animal-derived ingredients present. 6. glycemic_index set to 55 (analogous estimate from sucrose GI reference, University of Sydney GI Database); gi_category low. glycemic_load_per_serving calculated as (55 × 21.0 g carbohydrate per 500 ml serving) / 100 = 11.6, which is a medium glycemic load. 7. Serving size of 500 ml (500 g equivalent for water-based beverage) used for GL calculation. 8. Potassium (~37 mg/100 ml) and magnesium (~3 mg/100 ml) values are from USDA SR Legacy brewed black tea. 9. All micronutrient fields set to 0.0 where the approved source reports negligible or zero values for plain brewed black tea; these are confirmed zero or trace per USDA SR Legacy. 10. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all fields are either confirmed values or confirmed zeros from the approved source. 11. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes use closest_parent match type as no exact ontology entry exists for sweetened iced black tea (Teh O). 12. Large serving size (500 ml) is based on standard Toast Box and kopitiam large cup sizing.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171917 (plain brewed black tea) with sugar addition estimated from standard kopitiam preparation. Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for protein_g, fat_g, and all zero fat sub-fractions. Set glycemic_index to 55 (analogous estimate from sucrose GI, University of Sydney GI Database) with gi_category 'medium' and glycemic_load_per_serving 11.6. Populated all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Used only closest_parent ontology match types from the allowed set. All hard gates pass. Score 7/8 (q4 failed due to GI category mismatch).

Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, as per University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI <= 55 is low). Updated relevant notes and health context guidance to reflect low GI and medium GL. All hard gates pass and score is 8/8.
